next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 4

Nate spent the first five workdays of Dominic Altieri's life calling in sick from a flip phone he still used like it might explode.

By Thursday Ray had stopped sounding sympathetic. By Friday he had stopped pretending this was funny. Monday morning, parked outside Kassos Development with Dominic's key ring sweating in his hand, Nate listened to Ray's last voicemail again because there was nothing else to do except go inside.

Come in or don't, Ray had said. But if you don't, don't make me keep lying for you.

The office occupied the second floor of a brick warehouse that had once held machine parts or textiles or whatever else old American buildings held before someone stripped the guts out and called the result loft-style. A rusted loading door still sat on the side of the building like an old scar. Somebody at Kassos had leaned into the history just enough to make it decorative. The lobby had exposed brick, black-and-white photos of tugboats on the harbor, and a potted ficus that had gone gray at the tips from office air and neglect. Up on the second floor the effect gave out. Carpet tile, fluorescent panels, beige desktop towers under desks, the hot dust smell of computers that had been on since dawn.

He sat in the Civic for another minute with the engine off, listening to the metal tick as it cooled. The July heat pressed through the windshield and turned the car into a damp box. Dominic's shirt stuck to his back. Sarah had watched him knot the tie that morning, then stepped in and redid it without comment, fingers quick and practiced at his throat. She h...
open_hooks_to_carry: Navigating 2005 without GPS — the Rand McNally atlas, the physical strangeness of the pre-smartphone world
The strip mall: first sight of Eastbridge Plaza intact, alive, functioning
prev_tail_excerpt:
...hrough an ordinary July morning with Mort alive and reachable to him, unreachable to Nate. The only future available was Dominic's unless Nate blew it up and took everybody in it down with him.

The thought that finally got him moving was not hope. It was workload.

He went to the little desk by the computer, found a legal pad in the drawer, and clicked a pen until it worked. His first attempt at writing looked wrong immediately. Dominic's hand was broader, the grip heavier. The pen moved with a confidence Nate did not own. He wrote DOM just to see it, stopped there, then filled the next lines with the names he already had: Sarah. Ray. Tommy. Ma. Kassos.

Under that he wrote, smaller, `Listen more. Don't volunteer. Search everything.` He stared at `Don't` for a second, irritated by how relieved he felt to see the contraction there, as if even the legal pad could tell when he was faking it. Then he put the pen down and flexed Dominic's fingers until the cramp eased.

The apartment around him offered none of a crime scene's mercy. Crime scenes were inert. This place was active. The phone could ring any minute. Someone could knock. Sarah could come back because she had forgotten something and find him standing over Dominic's paperwork like a thief in daylight. Every object in the room held information, and every object in the room could also expose him if he mishandled it.

Nate looked toward the bedroom where the unmade bed still held the hollow of two bodies.

Then he picked up the flip phone, opened it with his thumb, and started learning how to be the dead man he had made.
state_deltas_required: Nate's need to understand his new geography — and the gravitational pull of the strip mall where his father is alive
Nate sees Mort alive for the first time in nineteen years. The abstraction of revenge gives way to the embodied reality of a living father he cannot approach.
